title: 1  Introduction
text:
This book documents the context and progression of work around creating a discrete event simulation (DES) of an air ambulance service.
It is intended to provide decision support, allowing certain aspects of decisions around the design and resourcing of air ambulance services to be explored.

title: 2  An Introduction to Discrete Event Simulation
text:
2.1 DES - A Plain English Summary
A discrete event simulation creates a simulated world in which a series of entities (such as patients) and resources (such as helicopters and critical care cars) can exist.
Inside this world, we can define the complex and branching pathways that a patient can take.
We use a combination of historical data
A key part of simulation modelling is (controlled) randomness.
We are able to run the simulation model tens, hundreds or even thousands of times with variations in the arrival times between calls/patients, the nature of patient calls, and various other semi0random elements (such as being unable to fly due to poor weather)
This is almost like choosing a point in time and viewing thousands of possible futures - of which only one will come to pass!
Across these many simulations, we can then start to answer questions about things such as
The additional power that exists in this technique is then being able to ask ‘what-if?’ questions and run the simulation again.
crumbs: 2  An Introduction to Discrete Event Simulation

title: 4  Current Fleet
text:
4.1 Comparisions between models
Benefits of the H145 over the EC135 include

increased space
increased ability to carry
improved interior layout for critical care

clinicians have access to the full length of the patient during transport

increased maximum all up weight (MAUW), allowing additional fuel to be carried and removing the need for refuelling prior to returning to base when operating at the extremes of the area
increased scope for additional people on board even when running with two paramedics and a doctor, such as

a patient’s parent or guardian
a trainee

more recent computer system/avionics (Helionix), including features such as

HTAWS (computer-based geographical mapping system providing the crew with visual and aural alerts of terrain changes)

more pleasant

While it is a heavier helicopter :::{.callout-note} Quantifying differences between the helicopters in terms of - time out for servicing - unscheduled time out
Also, while not mentioned in the literature cited above, is it reasonable to assume that this incurs higher fuel costs? :::

title: 4  Current Fleet
text:
4.2 Night vision operations
At present, both helicopters are equipped with night-vision equipment.
This allows the helicopters to be operated at night if crews are trained with night vision goggles (NVG).

4.2.1 Night landing sites
At present, night landings are only possible at surveyed landing sites that have sufficient lighting.

title: 8  Hemsbase
text:
8.1 HEMS Desk
The HEMS desk data can provide insight into incidents where a critical care (helicopter or critical care car) would have been allocated if they were available.
This can occur in several ways:

title: 8  Hemsbase
text:
The care resources are already committed on another incident
the care resource is offiline during operational hours
the care resource if offline outside of operationaal hours

title: 8  Hemsbase
text:
8.2 TARN Data
This data can provide additional insight into those patients who were not conveyed.
